overview.title: La guitarra de mi abuelo
overview.topic: A grandson learning guitar from his grandfather

notes.heading: Imperfect for habitual past actions with soler
notes.body: Soler + infinitive in the imperfect (solía tocar) expresses something someone used to do habitually. There is no clean single-word English equivalent — 'used to' is the closest. Soler is rarely used in the preterite; when you want to say someone habitually did something in the past, the imperfect of soler is the natural construction.

notes.heading: De repente as a preterite trigger
notes.body: 'De repente' (suddenly) signals a punctual interruption of the background scene. Whatever follows de repente almost always takes the preterite because the action is abrupt and completed. This is one of the clearest contextual clues for choosing the preterite mid-narrative.

notes.heading: Estaba vs. toqué in the same sentence
notes.body: When an emotional or physical state (estaba nervioso) exists as background and a single action then occurs within it (toqué las notas), the state takes the imperfect and the action takes the preterite. The imperfect is the stage; the preterite is what happens on it. This two-tense pattern within one sentence is the core of the preterite/imperfect distinction.

notes.heading: Narrative present tense as a closing anchor
notes.body: Shifting to the present tense at the end of a past-tense story (hoy, cuando toco) is a common literary device that connects past memory to ongoing present reality. It signals that the experience still matters now. Recognizing this shift — and not treating it as a mistake — is important for reading authentic Spanish narrative.

sentences.translation: When I was little, my grandfather used to play the guitar every night.
parts: tocar la guitarra todas las noches.
rationale: era → imperfecto: describes a continuous past state ("when I was young" sets the habitual backdrop).
solía → imperfecto: soler + infinitive expresses habitual past action ("used to play every night").

sentences.translation: One day, he showed me the guitar and told me that someday it would be mine.
rationale: mostró → pretérito: a single completed action on a specific day.
dijo → pretérito: a specific statement made at that moment, fully completed.

sentences.translation: The guitar was old and had a small crack in the body.
parts: una grieta pequeña en el cuerpo.
rationale: era → imperfecto: describes a characteristic/physical state of the guitar, not a change.
tenía → imperfecto: describes an ongoing physical feature — background description.

sentences.translation: My grandfather told me that he bought it when he was twenty years old.
rationale: contó → pretérito: the act of telling is a discrete, completed event.
compró → pretérito: a specific purchase completed at one point in the past.

sentences.translation: I was nervous, but I played the first notes carefully.
rationale: estaba → imperfecto: describes an emotional state acting as background context.
toqué → pretérito: playing those notes was a single completed action.

sentences.translation: My grandfather listened to me while I practiced and didn't say anything.
rationale: escuchaba → imperfecto: listening was ongoing/simultaneous background to my playing.
practicaba → imperfecto: practicing was also continuous; both actions overlap in duration.

sentences.translation: Suddenly, he stood up and gave me a big hug.
rationale: se levantó → pretérito: de repente signals a sudden punctual interruption — a completed action.
dio → pretérito: a single completed gesture at that moment.

sentences.translation: I knew that music united us in a special way.
parts: Yo sabía que la música nos
rationale: unía → imperfecto: expresses a general ongoing truth or state in the past, not a specific event.

sentences.translation: That night, we played until very late and then fell asleep together.
rationale: tocamos → pretérito: that specific night is a bounded, completed session.
nos dormimos → pretérito: falling asleep was a puntual completed event at the end of that night.

sentences.translation: Today, when I play that guitar, I always think of him.
parts: esa guitarra, siempre pienso en él.
rationale: toco → presente: the frame shifts to today — "whenever I play" — contrasting past memory with present reality.
